coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode colorimetric-fluorescence biosensor for endotoxin detection based on CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 nanomaterials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiulan
Li, Jitao
Yang, Dezhi
Xiang, Cheng
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Endotoxins are found in the outer membrane of all gram-negative bacteria and are a primary cause of endotoxemia, organ failure, and significant harm to human health. Limulus reagent as traditional detection method has certain limitations for the rapid and accurate detection of endotoxin due to the high costs associated with the horseshoe crab used in the sensing process. Herein, a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode biosensor was prepared for the rapid and sensitive detection of endotoxin. The chitosan-grafted Fe,Cu-doped carbon dots (CDs) was used to reduce KMnO4 to produce CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, which demonstrated distinctive characteristics including high catalytic activity as an artificial nanozyme and a fluorescence quantum yield of 76 %. On the one hand, CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 with the peroxidase-like activity and positively charged property can oxidize 3,3-diaminobenzidine tertrahydrochloride (DAB) and H2O2 to yield a brown product (oxDAB). Additionally, it can interact with negatively charged endotoxin through electrostatic forces, which leads to a reduction in its nanozyme catalytic activity. On the other hand, oxDAB can quench the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 fluorescence via inner-filter effect and static quenching. Therefore, a dual-mode biosensor that utilizes both fluorescence and colorimetric methods is developed with the aid of a smartphone. It was found that the gray value of absorbance increased linearly with endotoxin concentration in the range of 0.125–175 EU/L with a minimum limit of detection (LOD) of 0.058 EU/L, while the gray value of fluorescence intensity also demonstrated a linear increase upon the addition of endotoxin within the range of 0.05–90 EU/L with a LOD of 0.036 EU/L. The sensor is effectively utilized for detecting endotoxin in real injection samples, achieving acceptable recovery rates between 89.0 % and 101.3 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of no more than 4.51 %. This indicates the reliability of the fabricated biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
CS@Fe
Cu/CDs-MnO2
Endotoxin
Colorimetric-fluorescence biosensor
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and POD-like properties of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2
Determination of LPS in injection samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was strongly supported by Yunnan Revitalization Talent Support Program \u201CYoung Talent\u201D Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2020-247).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM image (insets: histogram of size distribution of 100 random nanoparticles and high-resolution TEM); (c) XRD pattern; (d) FTIR pattern; and (e) XPS survey scan of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2; high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) Fe 2p, (g) Cu 2p, (h) Mn 2p.

(a) Effect of KMnO4 for the fluorescence and POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs, (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence, UV–vis absorption spectra of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 catalyzing typical chromogenic substrates (c) TMB, ABTS, OPD and (d) DAB, respectively, (e) Effect of fluorescent to oxDAB.
Steady-state kinetic assay and Lineweaver-Burk curve of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+LPS for TMB (a,b) and H2O2 (c,d); ROS detection using (e) AA and ·OH scavenging with (f) MB.
(a) Zeta potentials, (b) Spectra UV–vis absorption and fluorescence of oxDAB and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, (c) Fluorescence lifetimes.

(a) Linear relationship between gray value and LPS concentration (inset: corresponding color hydrogel images); (b) Linear relationship between gray value and LPS concentration (inset: corresponding fluorescent hydrogel images), (c and d) Selectivity of the platform toward different interference. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and nanozyme hydrogel synthesis process.
The dual-mode for LPS detection principle.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode colorimetric-fluorescence biosensor for endotoxin detection based on CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Endotoxins are found in the outer membrane of all gram-negative bacteria and are a primary cause of endotoxemia, organ failure, and significant harm to human health. Limulus reagent as traditional detection method has certain limitations for the rapid and accurate detection of endotoxin due to the high costs associated with the horseshoe crab used in the sensing process. Herein, a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode biosensor was prepared for the rapid and sensitive detection of endotoxin. The chitosan-grafted Fe,Cu-doped carbon dots (CDs) was used to reduce KMnO4 to produce CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, which demonstrated distinctive characteristics including high catalytic activity as an artificial nanozyme and a fluorescence quantum yield of 76 %. On the one hand, CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 with the peroxidase-like activity and positively charged property can oxidize 3,3-diaminobenzidine tertrahydrochloride (DAB) and H2O2 to yield a brown product (oxDAB). Additionally, it can interact with negatively charged endotoxin through electrostatic forces, which leads to a reduction in its nanozyme catalytic activity. On the other hand, oxDAB can quench the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 fluorescence via inner-filter effect and static quenching. Therefore, a dual-mode biosensor that utilizes both fluorescence and colorimetric methods is developed with the aid of a smartphone. It was found that the gray value of absorbance increased linearly with endotoxin concentration in the range of 0.125–175 EU/L with a minimum limit of detection (LOD) of 0.058 EU/L, while the gray value of fluorescence intensity also demonstrated a linear increase upon the addition of endotoxin within the range of 0.05–90 EU/L with a LOD of 0.036 EU/L. The sensor is effectively utilized for detecting endotoxin in real injection samples, achieving acceptable recovery rates between 89.0 % and 101.3 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of no more than 4.51 %. This indicates the reliability of the fabricated biosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Endotoxin, commonly known as lipopolysaccharides (LPS), a highly pathogenic toxin liberated from the cell membrane of Gram-negative bacteria upon their demise, can induce severe disorders, such as sepsis, intestinal inflammation, diabetes, fever, septic shock, and even fatality [1]. Due to the risk of significant immune reactions, measuring LPS is a crucial aspect in guaranteeing the safety of sterilized products. International regulatory bodies, including the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), European Medicines Agency (EMA), Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency (PMDA), and National Medical Products Administration (NMPA), enforce stringent requirements for monitoring endotoxin contamination in the production of medications, healthcare equipment, and consumable goods to mitigate risks associated with their possible pyrogenicity and toxicity [2]. The Chinese Pharmacopoeia rules that the maximum acceptable LPS concentration is 5 EU/kg/h (50 fmol/kg/h) for injection, and 0.2 EU/kg/h (2 fmol/kg/h) for intrathecal injection [3]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of LPS levels is essential to ensure the safety of products that have been sterilized during production.

The limulus amebocyte lysate (LAL) assay, carried out in vitro, along with the rabbit pyrogen test (RPT), conducted in vivo, is acknowledged as standard techniques for assessing LPS levels [4]. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) and fluorescent methods are additionally utilized for the detection of LPS. Nonetheless, each technique has its particular limitations: ELISA encounters issues with the nonspecific attachment of LPS to the microtiter plate, whereas fluorescent detection requires specialized fluorophores [5].

Carbon dots (CDs), which are a category of zero-dimensional carbon-based optical nanomaterial, have garnered significant interest because of their ultrasmall size (less than 10 nm), outstanding optical and catalytic characteristics, low toxicity, and the simplicity of surface modification [6–8]. Nevertheless, unmodified CDs exhibits inadequate electron transfer, which reduces their catalytic efficiency and restricts their potential applications. Doping with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen (N), is commonly utilized to improve their internal electronic structure and fluorescence characteristics [9,10], and transition metal doping such as Fe, Ni, Cu, and Mn increases the active sites with excellent enzyme-mimetic activity [11]. Fe-doped CDs demonstrates greater peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic activity compared to CDs doped with other transition metals [12,13]. As a result, the incorporation of nitrogen (as a heteroatom) and iron (as a transition metal) enhances both the POD-like activity and the fluorescent quantum yields (QY) of CDs [14,15]. Chitosan (CS) has garnered significant interest due to its favorable biocompatibility [16], excellent degradability [17], non-toxic polymer [18], etc. Additionally, CS possesses a positively charged surface that enables it to adsorb onto microorganisms and interact with negatively charged cell surfaces through electrostatic forces [19].

Biosensors that utilize both colorimetric and fluorescence techniques can greatly minimize instrumental and human errors, thus improving the reliability of the sensors. Recently, dual-mode sensing strategies combining colorimetric and fluorescence methods have been created for applications in cell imaging [20], drug monitoring [21], and food detection [22]. Based on the advantages of dual-mode, we synthesized CS grafted Fe,Cu-doped-CDs (CS@Fe,Cu/CDs) via one-step microwave digestion and using citric acid, CS and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was subsequently fabricated using CS@Fe,Cu/CDs as the precursor and reducing agent to transform KMnO4. The nanozyme demonstrated remarkable POD-like and fluorescent activities, facilitating the oxidation of 3,3-diaminobenzidine tetrahydrochloride (DAB) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce a brown product known as oxDAB. Based on LPS could inhibit the POD-like activity and oxDAB quenched the fluorescence emission of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 via inner filter effect (IFE), a new colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode strategy is constructed for LPS rapid determination in injection samples (Scheme 1 ). This finding attributed to the binding of positive surface charge with negative charge on the outer membrane of LPS decreasing active sites of nanozyme. Additionally, we created a detection platform using agarose hydrogel to incorporate CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and TMB for on-site analysis of LPS in real samples. In comparison to existing methods, the proposed dual-mode sensor offers several advantages, including a broad dynamic linear range, high selectivity, ease of processing, and reliability.

A one-step microwave digestion method was employed to synthesize CS@Fe,Cu/CDs. Particularly, 0.3 g of CS, 0.3 g of citric acid, 0.172 g of CuCl2·2H2O, 0.180 g of FeCl3·6H2O, and 0.2 g of OPD were mixed into 30.0 mL of deionized water with 0.1 % (v/v) acetic acid (HOAC). After 30.0 min of ultrasound treatment, the solution was promptly transferred to a Teflon reactor and subjected to microwave digestion at 1 kW, achieving a temperature of 180 °C within 1 min and maintained for a total of 60 min. After the solution reached room temperature, it subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and subsequently filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate solid particles, leading to the collection of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs. The collected CS@Fe,Cu/CDs was subsequently freeze-dried using a vacuum dryer and stored in the dark at 4.0 °C in a refrigerator (The yield amount of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs is 69.06 %).

A 2 mg/mL working solution was prepared by dissolving the freeze-dried powder of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs in water. Subsequently, 40 mL of the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs solution was measured, the pH was adjusted to 4.0, and then 500 μL of a 10 mM KMnO4 solution was incorporated while stirring. After 1 h of stirring, an additional 300 μL of the same KMnO4 solution was introduced, followed by another hour of stirring to yield the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 solution. The resultant product was subsequently transferred to an ultrafiltration tube (with a molecular weight of 3000) for 10 min. Ultimately, Cu/CDs-MnO2 was obtained through freeze-drying (The yield amount of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 is 76 %).

The QY of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was determined following a previously established method [23]. In summary, a quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 (with a QY of 54 % at 360 nm and η = 1.33) served as the standard reference. The QY value was calculated using the equation provided below:
Q =
Q R
(
I S
/
I R
)
(
A R
/
A S
)
(
η S 2
/
η R 2
)
In this equation, Q denotes the QY, where the subscript 'S' refers to the samples and 'R' represents quinine sulfate. A indicates the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. I corresponds to the integrated emission intensity, and η stands for the refractive index of the solvent.

The POD-like activity was thoroughly evaluated through standard chromogenic reactions utilizing TMB, ABTS, and OPD as substrates in the presence of H2O2. The mixture was prepared by combining 100 μL of 5 mM TMB or ABTS or OPD and 100 μL 50 mM H2O2, 10 μL of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (1 mg/mL), and 100 μL of acetate buffer of pH 4.0 was mixed and incubated for 10 min.

In the kinetic experiments, assays involving CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 or LPS + CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 with TMB as the substrate were conducted by varying the concentrations of H2O2 (0.25–1.75 mM) while keeping TMB at 0.125 mM. When H2O2 is employed as the substrate, the concentration of TMB is altered (0.25–1.75 mM), and the concentration of H2O2 is fixed at 1.25 mM. The absorbances levels were assessed at 654 nm during various reaction durations within a 60 s timeframe. The Lineweaver-Burk plot was employed for the assessment of kinetic parameters (K m and V max):
1 v
=
K m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max
In the equation, v symbolizes the rate of reaction, [S] represents the substrate concentration (TMB or H2O2), K
m is used to represent the Michaelis constant, and V max signifies the maximum reaction rate.

As a ROS probe, ascorbic acid (AA) was employed to detect the alteration in absorption upon its combination with CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2. AA exhibits a peak of absorption at 279 nm. However, it is susceptible to oxidation by ROS resulting in the formation of dehydroascorbic acid [24]. In particular, 80 μM concentration of AA was combined with CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) in an HOAC-NaAc buffer solution at pH 4.0 and cultured at 37 °C for a duration of 30 min. The absorbance of the solution was subsequently recorded at a wavelength of 279 nm. Particularly, a concentration of 80 μM of AA was combined with CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) in an HOAC-NaAc buffer solution at pH 4.0 and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the absorbance of the solution was recorded at a wavelength of 279 nm.

Methylene blue (MB) was employed as ·OH probe by observing the alteration in absorbance upon combination with CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and H2O2. Among them, MB solution (1 mg/mL, 100 μL), H2O2 (50 mM, 100 μL), and CS@Fe, Cu/CDs-MnO2 (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) underwent a reaction in HOAC-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) at room temperature for 1 h.
Subsequently, the absorbance measurement was taken at a wavelength of 664 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

A nanozyme hydrogel sensing platform was fabricated. Firstly, 200 mg of agarose powder was dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water and subsequently heated in a microwave until complete dissolution was achieved. Following this, 500 μL of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (1 mg/mL) and 500 μL of DAB (5 mM) were combined and thoroughly mixed. Then, 150 μL of the resulting mixture was promptly dispensed into a 96-well microplate and cooled to room temperature.The fabricated hydrogel kit is subsequently stored at 4 °C for subsequent utilization. For LPS detection, varying concentrations of LPS (20.0 μL, 0.05–175 EU/L), along with 20 μL of HOAC-NaAc buffer (pH 4) and 10 μL of H2O2 (50 mM), were sequentially incorporated into the hydrogel kit and cultured at 37 °C for 10 min. The color or fluorescence was captured by a smartphone and further analyzed using the Color Assist App.

To detect LPS, different concentrations of LPS (20.0 μL, 0.05–175 EU/L) were added in succession to the hydrogel kit along with 20 μL of HOAC-NaAc buffer (pH 4) and 10 μL of H2O2 (50 mM), followed by incubation at 37 °C for 10 min. Under optimal conditions, appropriate concentrations of interfering substances were chosen, which included cations (Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+) as well as biomolecules such as β-d-glucose, pectin, glucan, glucose, sucrose, aspartate, serine, chitosan, and soybean oil.

According to the images obtained from TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 1 a–b and inset), the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 exhibited an average size of 5.10 ± 2.03 nm. The nanoparticle exhibited a nearly spherical shape and revealed a clear lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which aligns with the lattice fringes seen in the (100) planes of graphite carbon [25]. The XRD is depicted in Fig. 1c, the diffraction peaks at 12°, 17°, 20°, 21°, 27°, 30°, 33°, 35°, 40°, 41°, 48°, 50°, 52°, 54°, 57°, 60°, 64° and 67° belong to the (110), (200), (002), (110), (120), (310), (101), (400), (330), (420), (510), (411), (440), (530), (501), (521), (002) and (112) crystal plane of MnO2 [26]. The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 14°, 26° and 43° correspond to the (111), (002) and (100) plane of graphitic carbon [27].

The FTIR spectra of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 demonstrated (Fig. 1d) the peaks at approximately 1620 and 1700 cm- 1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the asymmetric CN and CO bonds, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1390 and 1080 cm−1 correspond to C–N and C–O–C stretching vibrations, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 3350, 2920 and 1539 cm−1 of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 correspond to the stretching of O–H/N–H, C–H, and the vibrations associated with N–H [28]. The peak around at 509 cm−1 in CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 could possibly result from the stretching vibration involving metal-O/metal-N [29,30], which is distinct from CS@Fe,Cu/CDs. To further validate the presence of each constituent and their interplay, XPS analysis was conducted on CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2. The survey spectrum presented peaks related to the elements C, N, O, Mn, Fe, and Cu (Fig. 1e), revealing the presence of element components in CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2. Furthermore, the proportion of each element in the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 is presented in Table S1. The XPS peak for the C 1s is detectable at 283.9, 285.1 and 288.4 eV (Fig. S1), associated with the CC, C–C and C–O bonds [31]. The peaks at 398.4, 399.7 and 401.3 eV correspond to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N (Fig. S2) [32]. The O 1s spectrum can represent by a single peak at 530.6, 531.8 and 533.2 eV, corresponding to metal-O, CO and C–O (Fig. S3). The high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1f) clearly records four distinct peaks at 709.9 eV, 721.1 eV, and 713.0 eV, 725.0 eV, which are attributed to Fe2+ and Fe3+ in CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 [33]. The characteristic peaks at 934.8 eV, 944.1 eV and 941.3 eV, 954.4 eV showed the coexistence of Cu+ and Cu2+ in CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (Fig. 1g) [34]. As shown in Fig. 1h, the binding energies at 640.3 and 651.0 eV are assigned to the characteristic peaks of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively, implying the presence of Mn4+ ions. In addition, the peaks centered at 643.4 and 653.9 eV are assigned to the satellite peak of Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and POD-like properties of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2
ce:section.ce:para: CS@Fe,Cu/CDs possessed both POD-like and intrinsic fluorescence properties, but with poor stability due to strong reducibility of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs. Thus, CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 with high stability was prepared by using CS@Fe,Cu/CDs as reducing agent to reduce KMnO4. Fig. 2 a illustrates that KMnO4 promoted the POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs, while it has a significant inhibitory effect on fluorescence, and the effect is negligible at 0.125 μM. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 demonstrated excitation-dependent behavior within the range of 370–430 nm, aligning with the typical characteristics observed in most carbon nanomaterials [36]. Furthermore, when excited at a wavelength of 370 nm, the peak fluorescence emission intensity was observed at 460 nm. The aqueous solution of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 appeared yellow under visible light but displayed a bright blue fluorescence when viewed under 365 nm (Fig. S4).

As illustrated in Fig. S5, no notable difference is observed in fluorescence intensities at 460 nm between CS@Fe,Cu/CDs and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, indicating that they possess the same fluorescent group. Additionally, the photostability of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was examined. The findings indicated that following 10 min of irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 remained largely unchanged, with around 90 % of the intensity preserved over a period of 15 days (Fig. S6 and 7).

The POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was investigated using ABTS TMB, OPD and DAB as chromogenic substrates. As illustrated in Fig. 2c–d, the three substrates exhibit varying degrees of color changes, and DAB not only generate prominent characteristic absorption peaks but also reduce the fluorescence intensity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 (Fig. 2e). Therefore, DAB was selected as the chromogenic substrate for this study.

A steady-state kinetic evaluation was perform to explore the catalytic mechanism of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2. The characteristic Michaelis-Menten curves and the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plots for both CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+LPS were analyzed under ideal conditions for the catalytic oxidation process involving of TMB and H2O2. Further analysis led to the acquisition of the curve that fits the K m and V max (Fig. 3 a–d). Consequently, the K m and V max values for CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+LPS are presented in Table S2. The findings suggested that the catalytic rates of the POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 are higher than that of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+LPS both H2O2 and TMB. Both the K m values of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 toward H2O2 and TMB are lower than that of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+LPS, showing higher the affinity between CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and H2O2 and TMB.

AA was utilized as a probe for ROS. Following co-incubation with CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, the absorbance of AA decreased across the board, with the most significant reduction observed in CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+H2O2 (Fig. 3e). The generated ·OH were determined using MB degradation, which the absorbance decrease at 665 nm. Fig. 3f shows the absorbance of the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2+H2O2 obviously decreased. These results have also verified CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 can generate of ROS.

The principle of the dual-mode detection for LPS is depicted in Scheme 2 . CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 catalyzes the reaction between DAB and H2O2 to generate a brown product (oxDAB), which leads to fluorescence quenching of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 via an IFE. The LPS could inhibit the POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, which reduced the generation of oxDAB and recovered the CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 fluorescence. The zeta potential plays a key role in assessing the stability of nanozyme systems [37]. A lower zeta potential, whether positive or negative, shows a higher tendency for aggregation or adsorption. Therefore, the zeta potential of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was assayed both before and after LPS adsorption. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and LPS were measured to be 17.6 mV and −13.7 mV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the addition of LPS leads to a decrease in the zeta potential, approaching zero. When LPS was absent from the system, the solution presented a brown hue and a weak fluorescent signal. Conversely, the presence of LPS in the system resulted in a pronounced brown color and an increased fluorescent intensity. Furthermore, there was a positive relationship was noted between the concentration of LPS and the variations in both color and fluorescence. This result suggested that the competitive binding of both oxDAB and LPS to CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 led to the activity of POD-like decreasing. Therefore, the dual-mode detection methods for LPS were established using oxDAB UV–vis absorption and CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 fluorescence as the analytical signals.

To further explore the interaction mechanism between CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 and oxDAB, we measured the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2. Fig. 4b illustrates that the emission spectrum of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 at 475 nm notably coincides with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of oxDAB at 466 nm, indicating a possibility for IFE and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [38]. Typically, fluorescent lifetimes and zeta potential are regarded as key indicators for differentiating between IFE and FRET. As demonstrated in Fig. 4c, no significant variation was observed in fluorescence lifetimes when oxDAB was present compared to its absence in the system, which confirming the non-existence of FRET. Therefore, it can be inferred that oxDAB is capable of quenching CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 fluorescence due to IFE.

To enhance detection sensitivity, a systematic investigation was conducted on the various factors affecting the POD-like activity of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, including pH, incubation temperature, concentration of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, and incubation duration. As illustrated in Figs. S8–11, the optimal values for the colorimetric and fluorescence sensors are 4.0 for pH, 37 °C for incubation temperature, 25 ng/mL for concentration of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, and 15 min for incubation time; while the corresponding values are 4.0 for pH, 47 °C for incubation temperature, 100 ng/mL for concentration of CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2, and 15 min for incubation time.

Optical images in both colorimetric and fluorescence modes were obtained using a smartphone under bright field and UV light conditions. The digital representations of red, green, and blue (RGB) values were analyzed with the "Color Assisted" app. The platform clearly demonstrated a gradual brown fading with increasing LPS concentrations, allowing for easy visual detection. By taking the RGB value and using the image processing software Photoshop 2019 for processing, the RGB value is converted to the gray value: Gray = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined using the equation LOD = 3σ/k, in which σ indicates the standard deviation of blank samples and k represents the slope of the created standard curve. The linear fitted of gray values against LPS concentrations can be automatically generated. The gray value exhibited the linear correlation (y = 0.874x + 68.986, R2 = 0.990) within the concentration range of 0.125–175 EU/L with LOD of 0.058 EU/L for colorimetric detection, and (y = 1.006x + 111.637, R2 = 0.998) within the concentration range of 0.05–90 EU/L with LOD of 0.036 EU/L for fluorescence detection. CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 as sensitive colorimetric and fluorescence sensors can be used to identify LPS in the injection samples based on the low LOD value (Fig. 5 a and b). 100-fold concentration of ions (Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+) and other interfering species (β-d-glucose, pectin, glucan, glucose, sucrose, aspartate, serine, chitosan, and soybean oil) with interference to limulus test method were selected as research objects. Fig. 5c–d shows that the absorbance and fluorescence intensity were almost unchanged. Consequently, the findings demonstrated that the sensor showed remarkable selectivity for LPS and could be employed to detect LPS in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LPS in injection samples
ce:section.ce:para: The established method was utilized to measure the levels of LPS in injection samples. The primary objective of this study was to assess the sensor's practicality and accuracy. Three injection samples had been spiked with 0.2, 20, 100 EU/L by UV–vis and fluorescence analysis. As can be observed from Table 1 , the recovery rate of the UV-vis absorption method based on RGB ranges from 90.0 to 101.2 %, and the RSD does not exceed 4.51 %. The recovery rate of the fluorescence method based on RGB is within the range of 89.0–101.3 %, and the RSD is less than 3.50 %. Moreover, there is no obvious difference between the measured value obtained by our method and the photometric method (Dynamic color development) for bacterial endotoxin test (Table S3). In comparison to the commonly used analytical methods, our developed dual-mode system demonstrated greater sensitivity and a broader linear range, as summarized in Table S4.

A fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform had been successfully constructed for bacterial LPS analysis based on CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 nanozyme. The CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 showed excellent both POD-like and fluorescence performances. The color change resulting from the catalysis of DAB by CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 acts as a chromogenic signal. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quenching caused by ox-DAB is utilized as the fluorescent signal. In the presence of LPS, the POD-like activity of positively charged CS@Fe,Cu/CDs-MnO2 was inhibited with negatively charged LPS by electrostatic interaction, leading to reduced oxDAB production and increased fluorescence recovery. In comparison with the currently employed LAL detection, the biosensor exhibits higher sensitivity and selectivity for LPS quantification of injected samples, and the reaction rate is rapid, thus it possesses greater application potential.

Qiulan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jitao Li: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Dezhi Yang: Validation, Conceptualization. Cheng Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yaling Yang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was strongly supported by Yunnan Revitalization Talent Support Program “Young Talent” Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2020-247).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.127330.
